title: Enterprise CMS
metaTitle: Enterprise CMS Strategy & Implementation · Artifact Digital
description: Enterprise CMS strategy, content modeling, and implementation — Optimizely, Sitecore, and composable/headless. We build content platforms teams can actually run.
lede: Enterprise CMS projects rarely fail because of the technology. They fail because the content model was an afterthought and the people who'd use the system daily were never in the room. We fix both.
summary: Content modeling, migration, and implementation on platforms teams can run.
intro: Enterprise content management is where a lot of ambitious digital strategies quietly go to die. A company picks a powerful platform — Optimizely, Sitecore, a composable stack — expecting it to solve their content problems. Eighteen months and a very large invoice later, the site is just as hard to update as before, editors have built a shadow system of workarounds, and everyone blames the software.

The software is almost never the real problem. The real problem is that the content model — the underlying structure of how content is defined, related, and reused — was treated as a technical detail instead of the foundation it is. Get the model right and a good CMS feels like leverage. Get it wrong and even the best platform becomes a very expensive way to publish a webpage.

We've done the large, painful version of this work: consolidating a sprawling six-thousand-page ecosystem down to a focused platform, unifying content operations across a dozen-plus business units on six continents. The lesson from every one of those engagements is the same. Model the content around how the business actually works, involve the people who'll live in the system, and the technology takes care of itself.

sections.heading: Content modeling is the foundation
body: Before we talk about any platform, we model the content. What are the real content types? How do they relate? What gets reused where, and what should never be copied and pasted? A good content model mirrors the structure of your business and your audience's needs — not the shape of last decade's website.

This is unglamorous work, and it's the single highest-leverage thing you can do on a CMS project. A clean model makes everything downstream easier: authoring, localization, personalization, migration, the next redesign. A muddy one taxes every future project forever. We treat it with the seriousness it deserves.

sections.heading: Optimizely, Sitecore, and composable
body: We work across the enterprise landscape — Optimizely and Sitecore among the traditional DXPs, and the composable, headless, and hybrid architectures more organizations are moving toward. Each has real strengths and real costs, and the honest answer to 'which should we use' is 'it depends on your team, your content, and your roadmap.'

We'll help you make that choice with clear eyes, including the parts vendors gloss over: the operational burden, the total cost past year one, the skills your team needs to actually run it. And if you've already chosen — or inherited — a platform, we make it work as well as it possibly can rather than relitigating the decision.

sections.heading: Built for the people who run it
body: The users of a CMS aren't your customers — they're your content team, your marketers, your regional editors. If the system fights them, they'll route around it, and the beautiful governance model on the whiteboard evaporates. So we design the authoring experience as carefully as the public-facing one.

That means sensible components, guardrails that prevent mistakes without smothering flexibility, and localization and workflow that match how your teams are actually structured. The goal is a platform people reach for, not one they resent.

process.title: Audit and model
process.body: We assess your content and operations, then design a content model that mirrors how the business and its audiences actually work.

process.title: Choose the platform
process.body: We match architecture to your team, content, and roadmap — traditional DXP, composable, or headless — with honest total-cost thinking.

process.title: Implement and migrate
process.body: We build the platform and move content deliberately, using the migration as a chance to consolidate rather than replicate the mess.

process.title: Enable the team
process.body: We design the authoring experience and governance so the people who run it every day are set up to succeed without us.

deliverables: A content model grounded in your business and audience, not your old sitemap
A clear-eyed platform recommendation, including cost and operational reality
Implementation on Optimizely, Sitecore, or a composable/headless stack
A migration plan that consolidates instead of copying the old chaos
An authoring experience your content team actually wants to use
Governance and workflow matched to how your teams are structured
whoFor: Enterprises whose CMS has become slower to use than the problem it replaced
Organizations facing a migration or replatforming and wanting to get it right
Teams weighing traditional DXP against composable and headless architectures
Companies with content sprawl across many business units or regions

faq.q: Should we go headless or stick with a traditional CMS?
faq.a: It depends on your content, your team's skills, and your roadmap — not on what's trendy. Headless and composable offer flexibility and clean multichannel delivery, but shift more responsibility onto engineering. Traditional DXPs give marketers more out of the box at the cost of some rigidity. We'll help you weigh it against your actual situation.

faq.q: Do you work with our existing platform, or only greenfield builds?
faq.a: Both. A lot of our highest-value work is making an already-chosen platform perform — fixing the content model, improving the authoring experience, cleaning up a migration that went sideways. We don't insist on starting over.

faq.q: How do you handle content migration?
faq.a: Deliberately, and as an opportunity. Migration is the rare moment when you're touching everything, so it's the best time to consolidate, retire dead content, and restructure around the new model. We plan it so you don't simply move the old mess into a new box.

ctaHeading: Is your CMS working for your team, or against it?
